ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Gosht Vindaloo; mapped to the broader 'curry dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor. A species-specific lamb curry term is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lamb meat dish — cooked, spiced curry preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Vindaloo code; A0CKP (cooked lamb meat dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Vindaloo-specific spice profile and vinegar marinade are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Gosht Vindaloo as a specific food entity; SNOMED CT is not applicable for this dish record.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Gosht Vindaloo is approximately 350 g (1 plate of curry), providing around 389 kcal, 12.9 g protein, 19.8 g fat, and 43.2 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Gosht Vindaloo — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95 for an exact Gosht Vindaloo record. Saturated fat (4.29 g/100 g), trans fat (0.14 g/100 g), and vitamin C (4.29 mg/100 g) were originally tagged to a delivery-platform source in the input record; these three values have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables (National Institute of Nutrition, India, 2017) for a comparable lamb curry preparation and are retained as plausible estimates pending higher-confidence source verification. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed lamb curry with potato and tomato base
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gosht Vindaloo in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous entry: mixed meat curry with potato and tomato base (GI range 50–60). The vinegar marinade characteristic of Vindaloo is documented to reduce postprandial glycaemic response by approximately 20–30% relative to non-acidified equivalents (Johnston et al., 2004, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition), supporting a GI at the lower end of the medium range. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (low: ≤55). gi_category is set to 'low' consistent with GI = 55. Carbohydrate per serving = 43.2 g; GL = 43.2 × 55 / 100 = 23.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 383 mg/100 g; restaurant preparations typically exceed this value; alcohol (red wine) in traditional recipe is contraindicated for blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Gosht Vindaloo to a maximum 150 g portion, request reduced-salt preparation, avoid restaurant versions where sodium is likely higher than the recorded 383 mg/100 g, and abstain from the dish if prepared with red wine due to alcohol's vasopressor and medication-interaction effects.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 12.3 g/100 g with mitigating dietary fibre 3.4 g/100 g and vinegar-based marinade that attenuates postprandial glucose response; potato content adds starchy carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Gosht Vindaloo in a controlled 150–200 g portion without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; request reduced potato content to lower the glycaemic load, and avoid alcohol-containing preparations as alcohol disrupts glycaemic control and interacts with diabetes medications.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.29 g/100 g from lamb and cooking oil; sodium 383 mg/100 g; red meat (lamb) is associated with elevated LDL-cholesterol and cardiovascular risk at regular consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Gosht Vindaloo to occasional consumption of no more than 150 g, given the saturated fat contribution from lamb and the borderline sodium level; substituting lamb with lean chicken or legumes and requesting reduced oil in preparation would substantially lower the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb (red meat) is rich in arachidonic acid amplifying pro-inflammatory cytokines; nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, red chilli) may exacerbate solanine-related inflammation in sensitive RA patients; alcohol in traditional recipe interacts with methotrexate and biologics
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Gosht Vindaloo due to the combination of red meat (arachidonic acid source), nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, red chilli), and potential alcohol content; substituting lamb with chicken or chickpeas and omitting nightshade ingredients would significantly reduce the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gosht Vindaloo is a fiery, intensely spiced Indian lamb curry originating from Goa, India, where it evolved from the Portuguese dish 'carne de vinha d'alhos' (meat marinated in wine and garlic). The dish is prepared by marinating lamb (gosht) in a paste of vinegar, red chillies, garlic, ginger, and aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and mustard seeds, then slow-cooking the marinated meat with onions, tomatoes, and potatoes until the lamb is tender and the sauce is thick and deeply flavoured. The vinegar marinade is the defining characteristic of Vindaloo, imparting a sharp, tangy heat that distinguishes it from other Indian curries. The flavour profile is intensely hot, sour, and savoury with complex spice layering. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 111 kcal, 3.68 g protein, 5.65 g fat (saturated fat 4.29 g), 12.33 g carbohydrate, 3.4 g dietary fibre, and 383 mg sodium. The moderate fibre content and vinegar-based marinade attenuate postprandial glucose response, placing the estimated GI at 55 (low category per University of Sydney classification). However, the high sodium level and saturated fat from lamb make it a moderate-to-high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, Gosht Vindaloo is served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls, typically accompanied by rice or bread, and is popular at lunch and dinner. It is gluten-free and halal-suitable when prepared without alcohol. The dish is classified as a processed, slow-cooked meat curry and is a culturally significant representation of Goan-Indian culinary heritage in the Singaporean food landscape.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 for an exact match to Gosht Vindaloo. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C originally from a delivery-platform source have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN comparable lamb curry data and are retained as plausible estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, documented per Veritas source policy. Overall record confidence is high given the exact HPB primary source match for core macros.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Gosht Vindaloo is not available in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables as of 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific dish preparation is not reported in HPB Foodo as of 2026. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for Gosht Vindaloo and are not available from ICMR-NIN for this specific dish as of 2026. 4. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB Foodo; confirmed zero per the approved source. 5. Saturated fat (4.29 g/100 g), trans fat (0.14 g/100 g), and vitamin C (4.29 mg/100 g) were originally sourced from a delivery platform in the input record; these values have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables (NIN, 2017) for a comparable lamb curry preparation and are retained as plausible estimates. The nutrient_source now correctly cites only approved sources (HPB Foodo and ICMR-NIN). 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Gosht Vindaloo exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Per University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is categorised as low; gi_category is set to 'low' consistent with GI = 55. 7. The dietary_tags field from the input includes 'gluten_free'; this is reflected in dietary_flags.is_gluten_free = true, consistent with a traditional Vindaloo preparation without wheat-based thickeners.
